plotlines.name: Hadji Murad's Russian Asylum & Family Rescue Effort
plotlines.description: Hadji Murad arrives at the fort under Russian guard with orders permitting contact with mountaineers via spies but forbidding exit without Cossack convoy. He lodges a week in Major Petrov's household, receives repeatedly bad news from spies about his family's captivity in Vedeno, befriends Butler, earns Marya Dmitrievna's respect, and departs still seeking a solution for his family.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Core protagonist arc — establishes HM's trapped position between two systems, the stakes (his imprisoned family), and the foundation of the tragedy that follows his departure.

plotlines.name: January 1852 Raid on Chechnya and Shamil's Counter-Campaign
plotlines.description: Per Nicholas's order a Russian detachment raids a Chechen aoul — Butler experiences it as picturesque adventure; the same aoul (Sado's village, where HM spent his last night) is shown next morning in devastation: Sado's son bayoneted, beehives burnt, fountain polluted. Chapter 19 shows Shamil returning from an unsuccessful counter-campaign and issuing a proclamation against Chechen defection.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Shows the senseless cruelty of imperial warfare via Tolstoy's double-POV reversal (16 glorified → 17 victims). Politically frames why Chechens both hate Russians and waver, tightening the vice around HM.

plotlines.name: Butler's Romantic Caucasus Illusion
plotlines.description: Butler, a Guards officer who fled Petersburg gambling debts, experiences war as poetic adventure, admires horses, avoids looking at corpses, buys a beshmet and imagines himself a mountaineer after befriending HM. His naive aestheticization of war and the Caucasus is the reader's lens onto HM.
plotlines.dramatic_function: POV character and audience surrogate; his romantic blindness contrasts Tolstoy's brutal reality. Provides the sympathetic Russian gaze on HM that humanizes him for the viewer.

plotlines.name: Butler's Restrained Attraction to Marya Dmitrievna
plotlines.description: Butler finds Marya Dmitrievna attractive, senses she may want him, but refuses to betray his comrade Petrov and maintains a simple respectful attitude. A quiet ethical micro-conflict that stays subtext.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Establishes Butler's moral fiber and the domestic tension at the fort; primes the later emotional weight of Marya's sympathy for HM.

plotlines.name: Marya Dmitrievna's Sympathy and Respect for Hadji Murad
plotlines.description: Marya moves from flustered curiosity (ch.18) to active service, interest in the spy-negotiations about HM's family, receiving HM's burka as farewell gift, and a final open defense of HM against Petrovsky's and the major's slurs: 'Though he's a Tartar he's still a good man!'
plotlines.dramatic_function: Humanizes HM through the eyes of a Russian woman; provides moral counterweight to military-political cynicism. One of few uncomplicatedly warm relationships in the novel.

plotlines.name: Major Petrov's Alcoholism and Domestic Life
plotlines.description: Petrov drinks himself to oblivion after the raid, wakes with a headache, asks his orderly for 'medicine' (vodka) before facing HM. Marya nurses him.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Atmospheric grounding of frontier Russian officer life; mild comic relief; sets up the contrast between Petrov's coarseness and HM's dignity.

plotlines.name: Destruction of Sado's Aoul and Family
plotlines.description: Sado returns to find his saklya ruined, his bright-eyed son bayoneted, his mother wailing with torn smock, beehives and orchard burnt, fountain and mosque defiled. Villagers decide to appeal to Shamil and rebuild.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Moral indictment of the raid shown two chapters earlier; pays off HM's earlier hospitality arc with Sado; establishes the collective repulsion that makes HM's tightrope impossible.

plotlines.name: HM's Family Captivity under Shamil in Vedeno
plotlines.description: HM's mother Patimat and wives are kept under guard at Ibrahim Raschid's saklya; his 18-year-old son Yusuf is in a 7-foot pit with seven criminals. Shamil sentences: if HM does not return by Bairam, the women will be dispersed to different aouls and Yusuf will be beheaded — commuted to blinding.
plotlines.dramatic_function: The ticking clock that drives HM's entire subsequent desperate action; raises stakes from abstract to visceral. Yusuf's attempted suicide after the sentence dramatizes the trap.

plotlines.name: Shamil's Return, Triumphal Staging, and Domestic Power
plotlines.description: Shamil returns from defeat he frames as victory, performs stony public piety, judges criminals per Shariat, dictates the anti-defection proclamation, decides HM must be enticed back and killed, then is petty-snubbed by his young wife Aminal who hides from him because he gave silk to Zeidat.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Mirrors Nicholas I's earlier court chapter — shows that tyrannical self-worship operates identically on both sides of the war. Essential for Tolstoy's thematic symmetry.

plotlines.name: Shamil's Plot to Lure and Kill Hadji Murad
plotlines.description: Council concludes HM must be killed — either by assassin in Tiflis or by luring him back via his son. Yusuf is forced to write a letter threatening mutilation/dispersal.
plotlines.dramatic_function: The hidden counter-engine driving the tragedy; creates dramatic irony for every HM scene at the fort that follows.

plotlines.name: Yusuf's Imprisonment, Interview and Attempted Suicide
plotlines.description: Yusuf — originally admiring Shamil against his father — is summoned from the pit, threatened with blinding, dictated a letter, then tries to stab himself with a guard's dagger and is returned bound to the pit.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Makes the family threat concrete and embodied; generationally dramatizes the cost of HM's defection. Cruelty of the sentence (blinding) functions as thematic peer to Nicholas's sentencing earlier.

plotlines.name: Hadji Murad & Butler's Kunak Friendship
plotlines.description: From first meeting (ch.18) HM takes immediate liking to Butler, consults him, shares spy news; Butler visits HM's room, listens to Khanefi's mournful song about blood-feud, adopts Caucasian dress. They part as kunaks (sworn friends) at HM's departure.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Provides the emotional bond that carries the reader/viewer through HM's final journey; externalizes HM's isolation and nobility in a sympathetic relationship.

plotlines.name: Khanefi's Blood-Feud Song (Thematic Micro-Arc)
plotlines.description: Khanefi sings a mountain song about a warrior's death in a blood-feud ('The earth will dry on my grave...'), which HM praises as 'Good song! Wise song!' — foreshadowing his own fate.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Foreshadowing device; compresses Caucasian death-culture and the inevitability of HM's end into a single poetic beat. High cinematic value.

plotlines.name: Arslan Khan's Blood-Feud Attack at Departure
plotlines.description: Tash-Kichu villagers invite HM to their mosque; Kumukh princes (blood-feud with HM) forbid it, fight the people; Russians cancel visit. At departure, Arslan Khan rides up, draws pistol, misses; HM springs cat-like despite his lameness, grabs bridle and dagger, is separated by Butler/Eldar/major. HM: 'if he kills me, such is Allah's will.'
plotlines.dramatic_function: Demonstrates HM's physical prowess one last time on screen; embodies the literal law of blood-feud that dooms him; final glimpse of his fatalistic courage. Mid-sized action beat, adaptable as a cinematic vignette.

plotlines.name: HM's Tiflis Experience (Offstage Referential)
plotlines.description: Petrovsky asks how HM liked Tiflis: HM liked the theater, disapproved of the ball dresses, delivers proverb about dog and ass giving each other wrong food.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Establishes HM's cultural dignity and the intractable incommensurability of the two worlds — the novel's core theme compressed into one exchange.

plotlines.name: Thematic Mirror: Nicholas's Court vs. Shamil's Court
plotlines.description: Chapter 19's depiction of Shamil — stony face, ornament-free austerity as performance, petty domestic pique, performative piety, brutal sentencing by religious code — explicitly mirrors Nicholas's earlier chapter.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Tolstoy's central structural rhyme: imperial Russia and Imam's Imamate are morally isomorphic. Removing this destroys the book's political thesis.

plotlines.name: Thematic Motif: War as Poetic Adventure vs. War as Atrocity
plotlines.description: Deliberate diptych: ch.16 sings war as diamond-peaks, songs, honeycombs, no wounded seen; ch.17 shows the identical raid from the victims' POV — disemboweled boy, dead bees, defiled water.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Tolstoy's most direct moral statement in this range — the author's lens flips to strip Butler's romanticism. In screenplay, this becomes a hard match-cut.

plotlines.name: Authorial Digression on Chechen Attitude to Russians
plotlines.description: Tolstoy states the Chechen feeling toward Russians is 'not hatred' but a repulsion 'such as ... the desire to exterminate rats, poisonous spiders, or wolves ... as natural an instinct as self-preservation.'
plotlines.dramatic_function: Pure narratorial framing; cannot be filmed directly but shapes the emotional key of ch.17 and any adaptation's treatment of the Chechen POV.

notes: Ch.16-17 form a tight diptych (raid from Russian POV, then victims' POV) — strong candidate for intercutting in a feature. Ch.19 is a dense Shamil-court chapter that structurally mirrors an earlier Nicholas chapter outside this range; the mirror motif must be preserved even under heavy compression. The Butler-Marya micro-attraction (ch.16/18) is the most cuttable single thread. 'characters_involved' lists kept to named agents; crowds (Cossacks, murids, villagers) omitted. Sado and his family in ch.17 are the same Sado who hosted HM before his defection (ch.I-II), so this thread has strong fusion value with the pre-range Sado thread.
